perturbation.original_text: Before providing access to Evaluation Material to any Representative, the Receiving Party shall inform such Representative of the confidentiality of the Evaluation Material, and shall advise such Representative that, by accepting possession of or access to such information, such Representative is agreeing to be bound by this Agreement. Each Party shall be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by any of its Representatives.
perturbation.changed_text: Before providing access to Evaluation Material to any Representative, the Receiving Side shall inform such Representative of the confidentiality of the Evaluation Material, and shall advise such Representative that, by accepting possession of or access to such information, such Representative is agreeing to be bound by this Agreement. Each Party shall be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by any of its Representatives.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Receiving Party' to 'Receiving Side' in the first sentence but keeping 'Party' in the second sentence introduces inconsistency. It creates an in-text contradiction because the text abruptly switches from referring to 'Receiving Side' to 'Party' without clarification. The question arises: is the 'Party' responsible only for the breaches of the 'Receiving Side', or also for breaches committed by any other entity that is still defined as a 'Party' under the contract.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Before providing access to Evaluation Material to any Representative, the Receiving Side shall inform such Representative of the confidentiality of the Evaluation Material, and shall advise such Representative that, by accepting possession of or access to such information, such Representative is agreeing to be bound by this Agreement. Each Party shall be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by any of its Representatives.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Receiving Party' to 'Receiving Side' in the first sentence, while retaining 'Party' in the second, creates an in-text contradiction. The contradicted text is identical to the changed text, highlighting the direct contradiction created by the inconsistent terminology. The explanation states that this inconsistency raises questions about who is responsible for breaches, creating ambiguity and a potential conflict in obligations. This aligns with the 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction' definition, as the contract uses multiple terms (Receiving Side and Party) without clear definition, leading to conflicting obligations.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party acknowledges that the Evaluation Material may contain material nonpublic information concerning the Furnishing Party. Each Party further acknowledges its awareness of the restrictions imposed by federal and state securities laws 2 on persons in possession of material nonpublic information. Nothing herein shall constitute an admission by either Party that any Evaluation Material in fact contains material nonpublic information concerning the Furnishing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Contractor acknowledges that the Evaluation Material may contain material nonpublic information concerning the Furnishing Party. Each Party further acknowledges its awareness of the restrictions imposed by federal and state securities laws 2 on persons in possession of material nonpublic information. Nothing herein shall constitute an admission by either Party that any Evaluation Material in fact contains material nonpublic information concerning the Furnishing Party.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the first instance of 'Party' to 'Contractor' creates confusion because 'Contractor' was earlier defined as Earthlink and PeoplePC. This introduces an in-text contradiction. If only Earthlink and PeoplePC acknowledge the possibility of nonpublic info, while the second sentence refers to any 'Party' acknowledging securities laws, it becomes unclear who is actually subject to those laws within the context of the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Each Contractor acknowledges that the Evaluation Material may contain material nonpublic information concerning the Furnishing Party. Each Party further acknowledges its awareness of the restrictions imposed by federal and state securities laws 2 on persons in possession of material nonpublic information. Nothing herein shall constitute an admission by either Party that any Evaluation Material in fact contains material nonpublic information concerning the Furnishing Party.
perturbation.justification: The change replaces 'Party' with 'Contractor' in the first sentence, while the subsequent sentences refer to 'Party'. According to the perturbation explanation, 'Contractor' is earlier defined as Earthlink and PeoplePC. This creates a contradiction because the first sentence applies only to Earthlink and PeoplePC acknowledging the material nonpublic information, while the following sentences about securities laws and non-admission still apply to 'Party', making it unclear whether all parties or just the contractors are subject to those laws and acknowledgements. This directly contradicts the meaning of the original text, as it creates ambiguity and conflicting applications of the clause. This aligns with the definition of Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: For a period one year from the date of this Agreement, neither Party shall, without the prior written consent of the other Party or the other Party's board of directors, either directly or indirectly through any affiliate or Representative or otherwise: (i) acquire, offer to acquire, or agree to acquire, by purchase, tender offer, merger, consolidation, share exchange or otherwise, ownership or control of any voting securities, or any direct or indirect right to acquire any voting securities, of the other Party, any subsidiary thereof, or any successor corporation thereto; (ii) make, or in any way participate in any "solicitation" of "proxies" (as such terms are used in the rules and regulations of the Securities and Exchange Commission) to vote, or seek to advise or influence any person or entity with respect to the voting of, any voting securities of the other Party
perturbation.changed_text: For a period one year from the date of this Agreement, neither Company shall, without the prior written consent of the other Party or the other Party's board of directors, either directly or indirectly through any affiliate or Representative or otherwise: (i) acquire, offer to acquire, or agree to acquire, by purchase, tender offer, merger, consolidation, share exchange or otherwise, ownership or control of any voting securities, or any direct or indirect right to acquire any voting securities, of the other Party, any subsidiary thereof, or any successor corporation thereto; (ii) make, or in any way participate in any "solicitation" of "proxies" (as such terms are used in the rules and regulations of the Securities and Exchange Commission) to vote, or seek to advise or influence any person or entity with respect to the voting of, any voting securities of the other Party
perturbation.explanation: Changing the first instance of 'Party' to 'Company' while leaving the subsequent 'Party' references unchanged creates ambiguity. The introduction of 'Company' without a clear definition, while still referring to the 'other Party's board of directors', it is unclear whether 'Company' and 'Party' are supposed to be distinct entities or if a 'Company' also has a board of directors. The in-text contradiction appears because the section initially restricts a 'Company' from taking certain actions against a 'Party'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: For a period one year from the date of this Agreement, neither Company shall, without the prior written consent of the other Party or the other Party's board of directors, either directly or indirectly through any affiliate or Representative or otherwise: (i) acquire, offer to acquire, or agree to acquire, by purchase, tender offer, merger, consolidation, share exchange or otherwise, ownership or control of any voting securities, or any direct or indirect right to acquire any voting securities, of the other Party, any subsidiary thereof, or any successor corporation thereto; (ii) make, or in any way participate in any "solicitation" of "proxies" (as such terms are used in the rules and regulations of the Securities and Exchange Commission) to vote, or seek to advise or influence any person or entity with respect to the voting of, any voting securities of the other Party
perturbation.justification: The original text refers to both parties as 'Party'. The changed text replaces 'Party' with 'Company' in the first instance but maintains 'Party' later in the same sentence. The contradicted text also maintains the use of 'Company' in the same manner, thus creating ambiguity because the contract uses multiple terms interchangeably without defining them, leading to conflicting obligations, as defined by the Perturbation Definition: Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction.
